name: ASM International N.V.

profile.description: ASM International N.V. is a leading supplier of semiconductor process equipment, specializing in atomic layer deposition (ALD), epitaxy, PECVD, and vertical furnace systems. ALD accounts for over half of equipment revenue and is the fastest-growing segment in wafer fab equipment, critical for next-gen GAA transistors and advanced memory. Founded in 1968, headquartered in Almere, Netherlands, with a market cap of approximately EUR 52 billion.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (3/3): ASM has transformed from a broad semiconductor equipment vendor into the dominant ALD platform company over the past five years, with ALD accounting for more than half of equipment revenue as the fastest-growing segment in wafer fab equipment [1][5]. Revenue grew from EUR 1.7B (2021) to EUR 3.17B (2025), a ~17% CAGR, driven by successful positioning at the leading edge of transistor architecture transitions (FinFET to GAA). AI Deployment (1/2): ASM is deploying AI for materials research and process optimization, training AI platforms on qualified content with 'promising results,' but no AI-driven products shipping to external customers [5]. Market Share (2/2): ASM is the undisputed leader in ALD equipment with the broadest portfolio of ALD products and applications in the semiconductor industry [5]. The company's technology is essential for gate-all-around (GAA) transistors, high-bandwidth memory, and advanced node transitions — chipmakers cannot produce leading-edge chips without ASM's deposition tools. Product Pipeline (1/2): Next-gen ALD and epitaxy tools for 2nm and below are in development with clear customer engagement timelines, though specific product names and launch dates are less publicly detailed than peer ASML [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): ASM's R&D spending consistently exceeds 10% of revenue, above semiconductor equipment industry average, reflecting the deep materials science innovation required for ALD leadership [4].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): ASM's purpose — enabling semiconductor innovation through 'innovative and disruptive technologies playing a crucial part in the AI revolution' — is specific, differentiated, and validated by 58 years of continuous operation through multiple semiconductor cycles [5]. CEO Hichem M'Saad's appointment in 2024 after 25 years at ASM demonstrates deep mission commitment. However, the purpose is technically focused rather than articulating a broader vision of societal transformation. Community Impact (1/2): ASM has committed to Net Zero by 2035 (among the most ambitious targets in semiconductor equipment), holds MSCI AA rating, CDP A/A for climate and water, and publishes detailed ESG supplements [4]. However, specific charitable programs or community investment dollar amounts were not identified. Employee Retention (1/2): CEO M'Saad joined ASM in 1999 and was appointed CEO in 2024 after 25 years — exceptional internal promotion. The cross-functional Executive Committee structure emphasizes talent development and culture [3]. No specific tenure or turnover data publicly available. Insider Buying (1/2): No evidence of significant net insider buying or selling was identified in recent quarters. CEO compensation is heavily equity-aligned, typical for European semiconductor companies.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. ASM's balance sheet is essentially debt-free with D/E near 0%, providing maximum financial flexibility [6]. ROE of 23.1% comfortably exceeds the 15% target. FY2025 revenue grew 13.1% YoY to EUR 3.17B, and the 5-year CAGR is approximately 15.2% [6]. Profit margin of 23% exceeds the 20% target. Q1 2026 gross margin of 53.3% and record adjusted operating margin of 33.1% demonstrate improving profitability [1]. The primary drags are Forward P/E of 39.38 (scoring 0.52) and estimated Forward PEG of ~2.6 (scoring 0.20), reflecting the premium the market places on ASM's ALD monopoly position. Weighted calculation (growth stock): D/E 1.0, ROE 1.0 (2x), RevYoY 1.0, Rev5yr 1.0 (2x), FwdPE 0.52, ProfMarg 1.0, FwdPEG 0.20 (2x) = 7.92/10 = 8.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): ASM International IS AI infrastructure — the company's ALD equipment deposits the atomic-scale thin films required for every advanced semiconductor chip, including AI accelerators (GPUs, TPUs, NPUs) [5]. AI cannot displace ASM; every AI chip fabricated at leading-edge nodes requires ASM's deposition technology. The transition to gate-all-around (GAA) transistor architectures at 2nm and below structurally increases the number of ALD steps per wafer, directly expanding ASM's content per wafer. Hyperscaler AI capex expansion drives chip demand, which drives wafer fab equipment demand. No analyst has flagged AI as a disruption risk; AI is purely an accelerant for ASM's business. AI Opportunity (1/1): ASM generates direct revenue from AI chip manufacturing — every leading-edge AI chip requires ASM's ALD tools, making AI a quantifiable and rapidly growing revenue driver as foundry customers expand AI chip capacity [1][5].

infinite_game.summary: ASM International demonstrates strong infinite game characteristics through 58 years of continuous operation, deep investment in ALD technology leadership spanning multiple semiconductor cycles, and commitment to Net Zero by 2035. The appointment of 25-year veteran M'Saad as CEO preserves institutional knowledge. However, the company operates in a capex-cycle-dependent industry where quarter-to-quarter order fluctuations can dominate narrative, and competitive positioning against Applied Materials and Lam Research sometimes tilts toward finite market share language.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): ASM's purpose of enabling semiconductor innovation through disruptive deposition technologies has been consistent for 58 years across multiple technology transitions — from early CVD to ALD to next-gen epitaxy [5]. The cause is genuine and has shaped strategic investment decisions (heavy ALD R&D commitment before it became mainstream). However, it is technically focused rather than articulating a broader vision of societal benefit beyond chip performance.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (2/2): CEO M'Saad's 25-year internal career at ASM culminating in the CEO appointment demonstrates exceptional institutional trust and knowledge preservation [3]. The Executive Committee explicitly prioritizes 'talent development and culture' as a strategic objective. ASM's culture of deep materials science expertise creates a high-trust environment where specialized engineers build decades-long careers. The orderly leadership succession and cross-functional committee structure reflect organizational stability.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): ASM's competitive positioning against Applied Materials, Lam Research, and Tokyo Electron focuses on technology differentiation (ALD precision and conformality advantages) rather than market share destruction [5]. Management's emphasis on growing the ALD market rather than taking share from competitors demonstrates improvement-focused competitive thinking. The company's acknowledgment that 'geopolitical tensions and competition' are risks shows mature competitive awareness.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): ASM has evolved its technology portfolio over time — from broad semiconductor equipment to focused ALD and deposition leadership, divesting non-core businesses (sold ASM Pacific Technology/ASMPT in 2022 for EUR 3.3B) to concentrate on its core competency [4]. This divestiture demonstrated willingness to shrink revenue to sharpen strategic focus. However, the company remains fundamentally a semiconductor deposition equipment maker — the core business model has evolved but not transformed.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): ASM's early and sustained investment in ALD technology before it became the industry standard required conviction and R&D spending through periods of uncertain returns [5]. The sale of ASMPT (a profitable business generating significant revenue) to focus exclusively on front-end deposition was a courageous strategic choice that sacrificed near-term revenue for long-term focus. However, these decisions were made from a position of technological strength rather than genuine sacrifice.

investment_thesis: ASM International delivered Q1 2026 revenue of EUR 862.5M at the top of guidance, with record adjusted operating margin of 33.1% and gross margin of 53.3%, driven by AI-led demand acceleration in leading-edge logic and memory [1]. Equipment sales rose 14% YoY in constant currency led by ALD, and management guided Q2 2026 revenue of EUR 980M (±5%) with H2 exceeding H1. ASM's monopoly-like position in ALD — the fastest-growing and most critical deposition technology for GAA transistors at 2nm and below — creates structural content growth per wafer as node transitions increase ALD step counts. The debt-free balance sheet, 23% ROE, and 15.2% 5-year revenue CAGR demonstrate sustained compounding. Full-year 2026 is expected to outpace the broader WFE market, with gross margins at the upper end of the 47-51% range [1].
thesis_continued: The primary risk is valuation and cyclicality. At 39x forward earnings and an estimated PEG of 2.6, ASM is priced for sustained double-digit growth, leaving limited margin of safety in a semiconductor downcycle [6]. Year-end 2025 backlog of EUR 1,247M declined from EUR 1,566M a year earlier, and the TTM book-to-bill of approximately 0.90 suggests orders are trailing revenue shipments — a potential leading indicator of deceleration [7]. ASM discontinued quarterly order disclosure starting Q1 2026, reducing investor visibility into demand trends. Customer concentration with a handful of leading-edge foundries (TSMC, Samsung, Intel) creates significant single-client risk. Geopolitical tensions and US-China export restrictions could limit ASM's addressable market in China, which contributed meaningfully to 2024-2025 revenue through mature logic/foundry demand. The stock price of EUR 1,066 trades near its 52-week high (EUR 1,080), suggesting limited near-term upside.
key_catalysts: Gate-all-around (GAA) transistor adoption at 2nm and below structurally increasing ALD steps per wafer, expanding ASM's content per wafer across all leading-edge customers
Q2 2026 revenue guidance of EUR 980M implying acceleration, with H2 expected to exceed H1 and full-year growth outpacing the WFE market
Net Zero 2035 ambition and 35% reduction in thermal energy per wafer target positioning ASM as the sustainability leader in semiconductor equipment
key_risks: Premium valuation at 39x forward P/E and PEG of 2.6 requires sustained double-digit growth; any semiconductor capex cycle downturn could trigger significant multiple compression
Year-end 2025 backlog declined to EUR 1,247M from EUR 1,566M with TTM book-to-bill of ~0.90, potentially signaling order deceleration despite strong revenue execution
Geopolitical risks from US-China export restrictions could limit ASM's addressable market in China, where mature logic/foundry demand has been a meaningful revenue contributor

backlog.note: Year-end 2025 backlog EUR 1,247M declined from EUR 1,566M. Q4 2025 new orders EUR 803M (up 26% YoY) show recovery but TTM B2B remains below 1.0. ASM discontinued quarterly order disclosure starting Q1 2026, reducing visibility. Equipment orders are direct customer commitments rather than IDIQ-style contracts.
